text: Chapter 4 Schooling, “peace,” and discipline The preceding chapters have described the theory and methods used in this study. In this chapter, we reveal the enormous gap between the discourse surrounding schooling and the discourse of anti-violence and conflict resolution activities practised in the everyday of the typical urban Canadian school. While there were a few unusual exceptions, this study revealed that the dominant practice of peacekeeping in our schools was characterized by practices of discipline, punishment, surveillance and gender and racial inequality and that these practices were firmly entrenched, at least in part because of the impacts of austerity measures and common core curriculum. This chapter examines how schooling as discipline can stifle any meaningful discourse of peace that could be truly democratic and social in nature, and the ways in which current practices of discipline and enforcement may perpetuate and even deepen racial inequality, which is a direct barrier to peace.
